From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Political party in Saskatchewan, Canada

Not to be confused with [Progressive Party of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Party of Saskatchewan").

The **Saskatchewan Progress Party** (**SPP**) is a [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"). It was founded in 1905 as the **Liberal Party of Saskatchewan**, and retained that name until members voted to change it in 2023. Until 2009, the party was affiliated with the [Liberal Party of Canada](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada").

The Liberals were a dominant force in Saskatchewan politics during the first half of the twentieth century, forming government for all but five years between 1905 and 1944. With the emergence of the [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party#The_Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party") (CCF) under [Tommy Douglas](/wiki/Tommy_Douglas "Tommy Douglas")' leadership, the Liberals spent the following two decades in Opposition before forming two more majority governments from 1964 to 1971. However, the party lost influence in the latter stages of the twentieth century. Although it reached Opposition status again in the mid-1990s, even that term was disrupted when much of the caucus abandoned the party to form the new [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") in 1997. The 1999 election marked the last time any Liberals were elected to the [Legislature](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Legislature "Saskatchewan Legislature").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Premier_Scott.jpg/250px-Premier_Scott.jpg)](/wiki/File:Premier_Scott.jpg)

Liberal leader Walter Scott was Saskatchewan's first Premier, holding the role from 1905 to 1916.

### Early history (1905–1944)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history (1905–1944)")]

#### Political dominance (1905–1929)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political dominance (1905–1929)")]

The Liberal Party dominated Saskatchewan politics from the province's earliest days. Saskatchewan entered Confederation in September 1905 alongside neighbouring [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"). Both provinces were carved out of the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"); territorial premier [Frederick Haultain](/wiki/Frederick_Haultain "Frederick Haultain") had advocated for the creation of one large western province called [Buffalo](/wiki/Province_of_Buffalo "Province of Buffalo"), but [Wilfrid Laurier](/wiki/Wilfrid_Laurier "Wilfrid Laurier")'s federal [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") government opted to create two provinces instead, wary of the potential strength of a province the size of Buffalo.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Haultain responded by seeking the premiership of Saskatchewan under the banner of his [Provincial Rights Party](/wiki/Provincial_Rights_Party "Provincial Rights Party"), but Saskatchewan voters opted in [1905](/wiki/1905_Saskatchewan_general_election "1905 Saskatchewan general election") for the provincial Liberal Party under the leadership of [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott_(Canadian_politician) "Walter Scott (Canadian politician)").[[3]](#cite_note-3) This marked the beginning of a long Liberal dynasty in the new Prairie province—the party provided six of the province's first seven [premiers](/wiki/List_of_premiers_of_Saskatchewan "List of premiers of Saskatchewan").

With the Liberals a strong force at the federal level, Saskatchewan voters appear to have preferred a provincial government that had influence at the national level, and there was crossover with Liberal premiers like [Charles Dunning](/wiki/Charles_Dunning "Charles Dunning") and [Jimmy Gardiner](/wiki/James_Garfield_Gardiner "James Garfield Gardiner") moving on to key federal cabinet positions.[[4]](#cite_note-4) As a consequence, the provincial Liberals presided over the province at a time of tremendous growth as a rapidly expanding population driven by immigration—the province became the third most populous in the country after Ontario and Quebec—established a large agricultural economy.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

One of the keys to Liberal success was their close relationship with immigrant communities and especially with the largest farmers' lobby in the province, the [Saskatchewan Grain Growers' Association](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Grain_Growers%27_Association "Saskatchewan Grain Growers' Association").[[6]](#cite_note-6) While United Farmer governments took power in both Alberta and [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") during the 1920s, and while the agrarian [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Canada "Progressive Party of Canada") performed well in Saskatchewan at the federal level, the direct entry of farmers into provincial politics was fended off for longer in Saskatchewan by Liberal leadership attuned to farmers' organizations. Early Liberal premiers were even known to invite key agrarian organizers to the cabinet table.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

The Liberals also stood against a rising tide of [nativist](/wiki/Nativist_movement "Nativist movement") sentiment in the province in the 1920s. While the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan_in_Canada "Ku Klux Klan in Canada") gained a foothold in the province—the organization had 25,000 members in Saskatchewan by 1929—and fomented discriminatory attitudes towards French, Catholic, and Eastern European settlers, Premier Gardiner defended his government's immigration policies and called the Klan both a foreign entity and a tool of the provincial [Conservative Party](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan").[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The Conservatives were reported to have aided Klan organization by supplying it with membership lists, and the party itself developed a nativist platform in the late 1920s, promising to protect British, Anglo-Saxon values.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The Liberals were also heavily criticized for years of blatant [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage"), which helped to move farmers closer to greater political participation and, more immediately, solidified a strong anti-Liberal bloc in the province. In the [1929 election](/wiki/1929_Saskatchewan_general_election "1929 Saskatchewan general election"), although the Liberals managed to win the most seats with 28, they fell short of a majority for the first time. They proved unable to gain the confidence of the [Legislature](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Legislature "Saskatchewan Legislature"), resulting in a coalition government under Conservative premier [James T.M. Anderson](/wiki/James_T.M._Anderson "James T.M. Anderson")—his party won 24 seats, and gained the support of the handful of [Progressive](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Party of Saskatchewan") and Independent members to form a [coalition](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") that they termed the "Co-operative" government.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Jimmy_Gardiner.jpg/250px-Jimmy_Gardiner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jimmy_Gardiner.jpg)

Jimmy Gardiner served two stints as Premier.

#### Opposition and return to power (1929–1944)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Opposition and return to power (1929–1944)")]

Anderson's government took power at the outset of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Canada "Great Depression in Canada"), which proved to be especially severe for the Prairie provinces and which provided the Liberals with ample opportunity to criticize the government's inability to contend with the crisis. In 1932, Anderson even invited Gardiner's Liberals into his coalition, but Gardiner refused.[[10]](#cite_note-10) At the same time, a new political party was gaining momentum in the province. In 1932, farmers with [United Farmers of Canada](/wiki/United_Farmers_of_Canada "United Farmers of Canada") voted to formally enter politics, and after a merger with the Independent Labour Party did so under the banner of [Farmer-Labour](/wiki/Farmer-Labour_Group "Farmer-Labour Group"); the new party became a founding member of the [Co-operative Commonwealth Federation](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_Federation "Co-operative Commonwealth Federation") (CCF) that same year.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The party was explicitly socialist, rapidly changing the dynamics of provincial politics.

Gardiner and the Liberals were able to return to power in [1934](/wiki/1934_Saskatchewan_general_election "1934 Saskatchewan general election"); the Conservatives failed to return a single member, and Farmer-Labour became the Official Opposition with five members to the Liberals' fifty. After the election, Farmer-Labour officially adopted the CCF party name. Almost immediately, Gardiner had to contend with the passage through Saskatchewan of the [On-to-Ottawa Trek](/wiki/On-to-Ottawa_Trek "On-to-Ottawa Trek"), a 1935 convoy meant to take the concerns of unemployed workers directly to the federal government. Prime Minister [R.B. Bennett](/wiki/R.B._Bennett "R.B. Bennett") declared that the trekkers would not proceed past [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan"), which acted as the headquarters to the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police"). Gardiner protested, predicting a riot.[[12]](#cite_note-12) He was proven correct on July 1 1935, when the Regina Riot led to two deaths and hundreds of injuries.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The Liberals held on to their majority in the [1938 election](/wiki/1938_Saskatchewan_general_election "1938 Saskatchewan general election"), but they lost twelve seats as the Depression continued and the CCF gained further momentum. Although the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") began to relieve Depression conditions, by 1944 the CCF, under new leader [Tommy Douglas](/wiki/Tommy_Douglas "Tommy Douglas"), appeared poised to take power.

### Varying fortunes (1944–1978)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Varying fortunes (1944–1978)")]

#### Opposition to the CCF (1944–1964)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Opposition to the CCF (1944–1964)")]

In the [1944 election](/wiki/1944_Saskatchewan_general_election "1944 Saskatchewan general election"), Saskatchewan elected the first [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") government in North America under Douglas' CCF. The Liberals were soundly beaten, dropping to only five seats. CCF organizing had effectively captured the Liberals' traditional base, with farmers and immigrant settlers largely moving to the new party.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The CCF's 1944 win marked the beginning of twenty years in government over five successive majorities. In Opposition, the Liberals became a vehemently anti-socialist party, persistently railing against the CCF government's interventions in the economy and presenting themselves as a free-market alternative. The Liberals staked the [1948 election](/wiki/1948_Saskatchewan_general_election "1948 Saskatchewan general election") on the slogan "Tucker or tyranny", referencing leader [Walter Tucker](/wiki/Walter_Tucker_(Canadian_politician) "Walter Tucker (Canadian politician)").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The CCF was re-elected to a reduced majority. In 1957, future party leader [Ross Thatcher](/wiki/Ross_Thatcher "Ross Thatcher") famously held a televised debate against Douglas in [Mossbank](/wiki/Mossbank,_Saskatchewan "Mossbank, Saskatchewan") on the topic of the province's [crown corporations](/wiki/Crown_corporations "Crown corporations"). Accounts tended to label the debate a draw, which was considered an achievement against the formidable Douglas.[[16]](#cite_note-16) All the while, the CCF focused intently on building a modern [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state").

The biggest battle between the two parties occurred over the introduction of universal health care in the early 1960s. The CCF effectively pitched the [1960 election](/wiki/1960_Saskatchewan_general_election "1960 Saskatchewan general election") as a referendum on the issue, and were re-elected to their fifth majority. However, the Liberals under Thatcher came firmly to the support of the province's organized medical profession, which was adamantly opposed to the scheme. Douglas resigned his post in the middle of this battle in 1961 to take on the leadership of the federal [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party "New Democratic Party") (NDP), and a [1962 doctors' strike](/wiki/Saskatchewan_doctors%27_strike "Saskatchewan doctors' strike") threatened to put an end to the plan. However, the strike was resolved and [medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(Canada) "Medicare (Canada)") was introduced in 1962, soon to be expanded nationwide.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

#### Thatcher government (1964–1971)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Thatcher government (1964–1971)")]

With the province divided over the bitter medicare debate, Thatcher was able to lead the Liberals back to a majority government in the [1964 election](/wiki/1964_Saskatchewan_general_election "1964 Saskatchewan general election"), although the popular vote was a virtual tie. Thatcher—who had begun his own career as a member of the CCF—promised to open Saskatchewan for business and to dramatically scale back the government's involvement in the economy. His biggest success came in the potash sector; while the industry got off the ground under the CCF, it expanded rapidly in the latter half of the 1960s, so much so that Thatcher ultimately negotiated a minimum price and production cap with American producers.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Although Thatcher had opposed the implementation of medicare, his government proved unable to reverse it with the program being rolled out across the country; Thatcher's government did, however, controversially introduce limited user fees for medical services.[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19)

Thatcher focused on downsizing the civil service, which was greatly expanded by the CCF. During the Thatcher years, many veteran Saskatchewan civil servants found themselves migrating to other provinces or to Lester Pearson's federal government—which focused largely on expanding the federal welfare state—and these migrants became known in government circles as the "Saskatchewan Mafia".[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Thatcher called an early election in [1967](/wiki/1967_Saskatchewan_general_election "1967 Saskatchewan general election") and won another majority. However, after the election Thatcher embraced a program of [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity"), reducing spending and introducing medicare fees, which became derided as "deterrence fees".[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19) A downturn in the provincial economy further eroded the government's popularity. In a [1971 election](/wiki/1971_Saskatchewan_general_election "1971 Saskatchewan general election") that the Liberals framed as a choice between capitalism and socialism, the Liberals were soundly beaten by a resurgent CCF—now called the NDP—under the leadership of [Allan Blakeney](/wiki/Allan_Blakeney "Allan Blakeney"), who promised a return to the CCF approach of economic planning. Thatcher died suddenly just three weeks after the election, leaving the Opposition leaderless for most of the rest of the year.[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19) In 1975, the Liberals were able to hold on to their fifteen seats and remain the Opposition, but for the first time in more than forty years, the Conservatives—now the [Progressive Conservatives](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan") (PCs)—won multiple seats.

### Political wilderness (1978–1995)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Political wilderness (1978–1995)")]

The resurgence of the PCs under [Dick Collver](/wiki/Dick_Collver "Dick Collver")'s leadership sapped support from the Liberals, and in the [1978 election](/wiki/1978_Saskatchewan_general_election "1978 Saskatchewan general election"), for the first time in their history, the Liberals failed to win a single seat, dropping to less than 15% support.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The result was even worse in [1982](/wiki/1982_Saskatchewan_general_election "1982 Saskatchewan general election"); while the PCs surged to power under [Grant Devine](/wiki/Grant_Devine "Grant Devine"), Liberals won less than five percent of the vote. Leader [Ralph Goodale](/wiki/Ralph_Goodale "Ralph Goodale") was able to return the party to the Legislature with his single seat in the [1986 election](/wiki/1986_Saskatchewan_general_election "1986 Saskatchewan general election"), a feat repeated by new leader [Lynda Haverstock](/wiki/Lynda_Haverstock "Lynda Haverstock") in the [1991 election](/wiki/1991_Saskatchewan_general_election "1991 Saskatchewan general election"). But for the better part of two decades the Liberals were largely on the outside of provincial politics. In addition to the PCs staking out a position on the right side of the political spectrum, space the Liberals had taken up firmly since the 1940s, this was exacerbated by the growing unpopularity of the federal Liberal Party in the province; the party's unpopular resource policies in the 1970s and early 1980s gave rise to a wave of powerful [western alienation](/wiki/Western_alienation "Western alienation") sentiment and damaged the Liberal brand. Moreover, Saskatchewan politics had become increasingly divided between perceived urban and rural issues, and the Liberal brand was particularly unpopular in rural areas.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)

Ahead of the [1995 election](/wiki/1995_Saskatchewan_general_election "1995 Saskatchewan general election"), the Liberals appeared poised to take advantage of a scandal-ridden Progressive Conservative Party badly damaged by an [expense fraud scandal](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Saskatchewan#Expense_fraud_scandal "Progressive Conservative Party of Saskatchewan").[[24]](#cite_note-:4-24) For the first time since the 1970s, the Liberals returned to Official Opposition status. However, the party's eleven seats to the NDP's forty two were seen as a disappointment.

### Dissent and decline (1995–2003)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Dissent and decline (1995–2003)")]

Dissatisfaction within the Liberal caucus led to Haverstock's resignation as party leader shortly after the election. [Jim Melenchuk](/wiki/Jim_Melenchuk "Jim Melenchuk") was selected to replace Haverstock, but internal strife continued. Some of the more conservative members of the Liberal caucus were in discussion with PC members about joining forces and forming a new party. Then, in August 1997, four Liberal members—[Bob Bjornerud](/wiki/Bob_Bjornerud "Bob Bjornerud"), [June Draude](/wiki/June_Draude "June Draude"), [Rod Gantefoer](/wiki/Rod_Gantefoer "Rod Gantefoer"), and [Ken Krawetz](/wiki/Ken_Krawetz "Ken Krawetz")—joined four PC members in announcing the founding of the [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party"). With eight members, the new party took Official Opposition status away from the Liberals.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In the wake of the defections, remaining Liberals tended to put into question how much the new party was a Liberal-Conservative coalition, suggesting that the Liberals who joined the new party were right-wingers resisting a move towards the centre that much of the Liberal Party felt was necessary. Like the NDP under leader Roy Romanow, the Liberals characterized the Saskatchewan Party as merely a re-branded PC Party.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In the [1999 election](/wiki/1999_Saskatchewan_general_election "1999 Saskatchewan general election"), the NDP were reduced to 29 seats, one shy of a majority, while the Saskatchewan Party surged to 25 seats, almost exclusively rural. The Liberals' four seats gave them the balance of power, and Melenchuk agreed to enter into a coalition government with Romanow along with fellow Liberals [Ron Osika](/wiki/Ron_Osika "Ron Osika") and [Jack Hillson](/wiki/Jack_Hillson "Jack Hillson"). While Melenchuk hoped that the move would allow the Liberals to regain some influence, he found the Romanow government itself was quite centrist.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) The coalition also divided the party again; Melenchuk was removed as leader—he and coalition partner Osika would contest the [2003 election](/wiki/2003_Saskatchewan_general_election "2003 Saskatchewan general election") as NDP members—and was replaced by [David Karwacki](/wiki/David_Karwacki "David Karwacki") when he defeated Hillson, who himself had withdrawn from the coalition. Karwacki ordered Melenchuk and Osika to withdraw the coalition, but they refused.

### Recent history (2003–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Recent history (2003–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Saskatchewan_Liberal_Party_Logo.svg/250px-Saskatchewan_Liberal_Party_Logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Saskatchewan_Liberal_Party_Logo.svg)

Party logo used c. 2009–2022.

Karwacki saw an opportunity to become the more left-wing alternative to the Saskatchewan Party. However, he took over a party that had lost much of its membership and financial backing.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) The party was shut out of the Legislature in both the [2003](/wiki/2003_Saskatchewan_general_election "2003 Saskatchewan general election") and [2007](/wiki/2007_Saskatchewan_general_election "2007 Saskatchewan general election") elections as the province returned to a two-party system now dominated by the NDP and the Saskatchewan Party;[[24]](#cite_note-:4-24) Liberal support dropped from over 20% in 1999 to below 10% in 2007. The losses sparked discussion of Karwacki's future as leader.[[27]](#cite_note-27) He resigned before the end of 2007, with party president Frank Proto taking over on an interim basis.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) This marked the beginning of an accelerated period of decline in which the party continued to lose support at the polls. Ryan Bater was introduced as the new Liberal leader in February 2009; at the same time, the party voted to disaffiliate itself from the federal Liberal Party, ending an affiliation that dated back more than a century. This did not change the party's fortunes; in the [2011 election](/wiki/2011_Saskatchewan_general_election "2011 Saskatchewan general election"), the Liberals ran only nine candidates and support fell to below one percent, with the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Green Party of Saskatchewan") surpassing the Liberals in popular support. Bater resigned as leader on January 31, 2012, and Greg Gallager was appointed interim leader.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) In 2013, Reid Hill was set to be acclaimed the party's new leader as the only declared candidate. However, he decided not to take the position, stating that he had hoped for a competitive race to revive public interest in the party, rather than simply being handed the leadership due to lack of interest.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Darrin Lamoureux was appointed interim leader on December 16, 2013, and was acclaimed the leader on August 21, 2014, when no other candidates ran for the position.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The party managed to field a full slate of 61 candidates for the [2016 general election](/wiki/2016_Saskatchewan_general_election "2016 Saskatchewan general election"), and they finished third among parties with 3.6% support. However, they were once again shut out of the Legislature.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Lamoureux resigned as party leader on September 9, 2017, and Tara Jijian was appointed interim leader.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Former NDP candidate Naveed Anwar was acclaimed as party leader on May 5, 2018.[[36]](#cite_note-36) However, Anwar resigned as leader on September 9, 2020, just weeks before the [2020 election](/wiki/2020_Saskatchewan_general_election "2020 Saskatchewan general election").[[37]](#cite_note-Tank-37) The party had again been set back by in-fighting, with Anwar and the executive at odds over election candidates and the state of the party.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) Robert Rudachyk was appointed as the party's interim leader for the election.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Running only three candidates, the party received just 355 votes, marking the worst performance in party history.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)

#### Saskatchewan Progress Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Saskatchewan Progress Party")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Accountabilitysk2.jpg/250px-Accountabilitysk2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Accountabilitysk2.jpg)

Jeff Walters led the party from 2021 to 2023 and led re-branding of the party to the Saskatchewan Progress Party.

Following the 2020 provincial election, the party set about looking for a new permanent leader. In 2021, members unanimously acclaimed [University of Regina](/wiki/University_of_Regina "University of Regina") lecturer Jeff Walters as the new leader.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Walters emphasized the need in the province for a centrist alternative to the main parties, as well as the distinction of his 'prairie Liberalism' from that of the federal Liberals.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Walters worked to increase his party's visibility in challenging the Saskatchewan Party on its handling of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Saskatchewan "COVID-19 pandemic in Saskatchewan"). On February 5, 2022, Walters organized a rally in front of the Legislature to oppose the end of COVID-19 mandates.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Walters also launched "Accountability Saskatchewan", collecting signatures to trigger a plebiscite on a public inquiry into the government's handling of the pandemic.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The petition was initially available only online, provoking an official ruling from [Elections Saskatchewan](/wiki/Elections_Saskatchewan "Elections Saskatchewan") affirming the validity of electronic signatures for this purpose in Saskatchewan.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In March 2023, party members voted to change the party name in order to distance themselves from the non-affiliated federal Liberals.[[44]](#cite_note-44) In July 2023, the party announced its rebranding as the Saskatchewan Progress Party. Walters stated that the new name was intended to "portray ourselves as visionaries", and that the party hoped to be a "post-partisan or post-ideological party".[[45]](#cite_note-:0-45) He stated an intention to focus on finance reform, government accountability, and 'green' energy.[[45]](#cite_note-:0-45)

In September 2023, Walters announced his resignation as leader and his intent to step away from politics.[[46]](#cite_note-46) He remained on as interim leader of the party until the scheduled leadership election in November 2023.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Walters later ran in [Regina—Wascana](/wiki/Regina%E2%80%94Wascana "Regina—Wascana") at the [2025 Canadian federal election](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_election "2025 Canadian federal election"), placing second.
Walters ran for the federal Liberals despite having supported the change of name of the provincial party to distance itself from the federal Liberals.

After the scheduled election failed to produce a new party leader, Nathan Bruce was listed as the interim leader in December 2023.[[49]](#cite_note-ElectionsSaskatchewan-49) In January 2024, Teunis Peters was listed as the party's new interim leader.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) None of those changes improved party fortunes; the party again nominated only three candidates ahead of the [2024 provincial election](/wiki/2024_Saskatchewan_general_election "2024 Saskatchewan general election"), and received only a few hundred votes for the second consecutive election, by far the fewest among registered parties.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Ideology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ideology")]

The Saskatchewan Progress Party has undergone a number of ideological shifts over its history, often responding to political developments around it. For the first few decades of the party's existence, the Saskatchewan Liberals were relatively closely aligned with the Canadian liberalism espoused by the federal Liberal Party.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Liberal Prime Minister [Mackenzie King](/wiki/Mackenzie_King "Mackenzie King") even successfully represented the Saskatchewan district of [Prince Albert](/wiki/Prince_Albert_(federal_electoral_district) "Prince Albert (federal electoral district)") in [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") from 1926 to 1946. However, the emergence of the Co-operative Commonwealth Federation as a political force in the province led to a rightward shift for the Liberals, as they began fighting elections as an explicitly free-enterprise and anti-socialist party.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The re-emergence of the Progressive Conservative Party in the 1970s forced another re-evaluation for the Liberals. By the 1990s, the party was attempting a more centrist approach between those of the PCs and the NDP; however, the Liberal caucus itself proved divided between left- and right-leaning members, leading to a split that helped create the Saskatchewan Party.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) However, another major factor in Liberal party fortunes has been a continued association with the federal Liberals, even after they disaffiliated in 2009. This has been seen as a particularly difficult for the party since the 1970s, when federal Liberal policies inflamed expressions of western alienation.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In recent years, the party has emphasized itself as a centrist party. However, political observers have noted that early in the twenty-first century, Saskatchewan parties had "crowded towards... the ideological centre", while more recent years have been marked by political polarization.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) The party's most recent permanent leader, Jeff Walters, suggested that the re-branded Saskatchewan Progress Party strove to become "post-ideological", emphasizing the need to pursue policy that "gives the greatest good to the greatest number of people".[[45]](#cite_note-:0-45)

### Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Progress_Party&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Notes")]

:   [**a**](#ref_1929a) The Liberals secured the most seats in 1929, but they were short of a majority and failed to secure the confidence of the Legislature. This enabled a coalition government to form and made the Liberals the Official Opposition.
:   [**b**](#ref_1995b) The Liberals began this Legislature as the Opposition. However, four Liberal MLAs left the party in 1997 to help form the Saskatchewan Party, which immediately surpassed the Liberals in size and took over as the Opposition.
:   [**c**](#ref_1999c) Although initial reports had the Liberals winning four seats, the result in [Wood River](/wiki/Wood_River_(electoral_district) "Wood River (electoral district)") was ultimately overturned by the courts and the ensuing by-election was won by the Saskatchewan Party candidate, leaving the Liberals with three seats.
